title: Stumbling Toward the Island
subtitle: A Life of Catastrophes, Blessings, and the Path to Liberation
epigraph: Just think, if all this, one day, all this one day, one fine day, just think, if one day, one fine day, all this stopped.
paragraphs: I want to begin by thanking all those people who have organised this lovely little celebration for me. The organising committee: Aya and Richard and Deya and Mark and Sarah. All those who've made generous donations to buy the statue that will go at the back of the pond, and to fund my little trip to Japan. I was relieved to see that I could get a return ticket. Thanks also to Charles and Heather and Mark and Peter, who've done this very professional recording for us. And of course, everybody who's chipped in for today.

I'm delighted that you've taken such joy in my own little birthday. But of course I myself am depressed. Yes, well, this is the beginning of the steep downward trend to decrepitude and senility. There was a card which some thought was telling me that seventy was the new thirty. It's not. Seventy's serious.

Something peculiar has happened to me this year. Suddenly—I suppose it's reaching the end of all this building work here, too, and feeling very settled outside Satipañña—memories of my past life came up. I mean this one. My past living. And I found myself wondering how it is that I got to this place.
It seemed an opportunity, really, to answer the questions I'm often asked but usually palm off with a sentence or two. How I became a Buddhist, how I became a monk, and why I continue.

To start at the beginning: my father was in the RAF. They were following the armies up the Tour of Italy when he was billeted on my mother's house, and they fell in love. It seems he took AWOL in order to marry her. When he got back to Cyprus, they put him in jail for a while. It's all very romantic.

He brought her to Britain, and that's when her real depression began. Imagine moving from a hillside village overlooking the Adriatic, with the smell of pine in your nostrils, and ending up in Manchester. Black Manchester. We forget how black the cities were—saturated with soot everywhere, and pea-soup fog.

She happily became pregnant with me, but decided to go home to have me. When the time came, they brought in the local midwife. My mother was twenty-five, so presumably there was some difficulty, but either they rushed it or it was the midwife's particular way of doing things—they cut her. Such was the agony that she bit into my uncle's thumb, and he showed me the scar. Even then I wouldn't come out. They had to prise me out with forceps. What a start. What a start.
She went on to give birth to two more sons and a daughter. So I've got two brothers and a lovely sister.

Consider the effect of birth upon us. The Buddha: as soon as he was born, his mother died. So there must be a feeling there of loss, whether the baby's fully aware of it or not. There must be a feeling of loss.
According to certain disciplines like rebirthing—that breathing thing—and Stan Grof's holotropic breathing, the birth trauma has a real effect upon us. It's there within our psyche.
I know this from the difference between my siblings. My mother said that my sister, who was last, eleven years on, fell out. And she's utterly phlegmatic. Nothing like me. So that was my birth.

Now, I want you to imagine this little five-year-old boy being taken to church by his father. He's walking into church. The church is quite big—St. Patrick's, belonging to a third generation of Irish. As you walk in, your nostrils are suffused with incense, frankincense. The atmosphere is very warm. And the people are very quiet.

You're taken up into the gallery, into the choir loft, where your father is conducting. At the point where the priest enters in his wonderful garments, followed by all the altar boys—because it was high mass—you're there as a little child, and you're let loose. You can wander around. You hang on to the barrier of the choir, looking down into the congregation, and suddenly Bach or Handel or sweet music comes pouring in.

This was my introduction to devotion. I was in ecstasy. And it's something I think a lot of children these days don't get—not that they don't get it with other things, like pop stars. But it was my introduction to religion as a real heart-based experience. And this was to carry on.
If my first catastrophe was birth, this was my first blessing.

My mother, being a southern European Catholic—they were Roman Catholics, so I should mention that—it was all about the love of Jesus for her. My father was all hellfire and brimstone. I had a nice balance, though I obviously warmed towards my mother. Northern Catholicism is very Protestant.

When you convert to a religion, when you actually move into a religion, you move in for various reasons. Most people who come to Buddhism come because they're suffering, because things have gone wrong and they've heard about meditation. But to actually develop a heart attachment—a good attachment; not all attachments are evil—a relationship of the heart with your religion is very difficult, because this is done when you're a child.

When you go to Thailand, you'll see little children brought into the monastery, into the temples. They bow, and there's the sandalwood and incense, and the chanting. That sense of devotion, which is a beautiful emotional state, a mental state of praise, of being in a lovely spaced-out place—it's absolutely glorious. And of course you can develop these things into absorptions.

That's very difficult for us because it's a different culture. We haven't got that sort of background. So I always think the way into a devotional, a heart connection with the Buddhist teaching, is through gratitude. The more you consider what the teaching has done for you and for others, the more this gratitude comes up. And it's very simple to go from gratitude towards love, towards a relationship.

Now, of course, the Buddha is gone. It's not as though we're relating to the Buddha. What we're relating to is an image within us of the Buddha. And in growing that, in a sense, we're forming a devotional relationship to the Buddha within us that we want to become. So it's the Buddha as he appears in daily life—not the transcendent Buddha, but the Buddha of compassion, of love, of joy. That's why I've got all these statues. That's the idea of devotion.

Anyway, we moved then to North Manchester, which was all fields at that time—out of Deangate Mostyn into the lovely fields. The fields didn't stay fields long. They were soon built on. And right at the back of us, they established a cemetery.

Then, while I was at primary school, a priest turned up. He gave us this wonderful talk with pictures about the missions. He showed us pictures of Flores, a beautiful island in Indonesia. He showed us pictures of New Guinea. And he showed us pictures of South America. He talked about the wonderful things that missionaries did out there. And if anybody felt drawn to that sort of thing, they should get in contact.

I got the 11-plus and went to Xaverian College, which was a grammar school. But two of my friends from that school went to St. Richard's College. And during that year, for some reason, it came to me that that's what I wanted to do. My mother was fiercely against it, but my father was like that, and it was my Aunt Rose who really wanted a priest in the family.

Come the age of twelve, I'm off on the train down to St. Richard's College. For me, it was entering into two years of undiluted heaven, because it was all about doing exactly what you want to do. And that's when you're most happy, isn't it? When you're doing exactly what you want to do in an unpressured situation, you're in perfect joy. Correct? For instance, a hobby.

The routine there was up about half past six, seven o'clock. We went down for mass, then out in most weathers—only in the most severe did we stay in—to do the old army exercises. Back in for breakfast, clean up, and school from nine o'clock all the way through to five. All that time, apart from a little break at lunch, was spent in silence. Can you imagine that for a twelve-year-old these days?
And I really got into it. All I remember is just being blissed out—and hating going home, having to come back, because it was just there that I found my joy.

Then something began to happen which promised a different sort of joy. It promised gold, but it turned into glitter. And the joy it promised finally ended up with frustration. Puberty. Even Mick Jagger could get no satisfaction. And he tried.

Anyway, we were let out every so often to go for a walk on a Sunday. There were about five or six of us on this particular trip, and as we got down the road, we suddenly had this idea that we'd smoke. Now, teenagers are by nature rebellious. The order didn't smoke—it was a German order, begun by a German priest, mainly German and American. The joke was: we don't smoke, so we drink. S-V-D.

So it was a little rebellion to go and get some cigarettes. And we decided to steal them. There was an old lady who ran a shop at this bridge on the Worcester Canal. We'd had it all figured out: somebody distracted her while somebody else took the cigarettes. I can't remember what my job was. And I daren't. So I'll leave that.

Well, we smoked, of course. And when we got back in the evening after lunch, we were all called to the prefect's office—the headmaster prefect. He asked us, had we taken cigarettes from this lady? We all said yes. She was no fool, eh? We were probably the only customers the whole day. The place was in the middle of nowhere.
So we were dismissed, and we thought, well, that's it. There was no corporal punishment in the seminary at all. If I'd been at Xaverian, you'd have been beaten.

Anyway, about a day or so later, we were all called for a talk in this big classroom. The rector, who was in charge of the whole place, announced that certain boys had stolen. He called our names and said, you are expelled.
Can you imagine that? I was three months off my GCEs, and they kicked me out.

I got back, and because of all that, my GCEs went all over the place. I ended up with about four. I had to take my English again. And unfortunately, because the education was mainly by Americans—who I don't think really understood the English GCE system at the time—I ended up with subjects I had virtually no interest in: Latin, French, and history. I had a bit of interest in history. So life went on, and I didn't do well.

I remember talking to my father about this. When I was thrown out, that was a cutting off of a vocation. This vocation to be a priest remained in me as a really strong, tremendous urge for three years, till I was over nineteen.
Why couldn't I go back? Because they never forgave me.

I can't tell you how important it is to forgive your enemies. When I turned up for my GCEs, they gave me a room in an outer building. I had to enter the exam room through a different entrance from everybody else. I wasn't allowed to talk to anyone, and they weren't allowed to talk to me. In other words, I was excommunicated. And there was no way back from that.
If a priest or somebody had come up and said, look, give it a couple of years and then you can join another order—I'd have had an in. But I had this block. I couldn't move.

So if you have any pity on your enemies, on all of them, do forgive them—because it allows them to forgive themselves. And of course, in offering forgiveness, we empty our own hearts of the revenge and the hurt. Now, it doesn't mean you've got to have somebody's forgiveness in order to forgive yourself, but it makes it much easier.

So I went on till about nineteen, and my father pointed out to me that since I was lost—I had no clue what to do with my life at this point, completely lost—why didn't I teach? He said I could travel with teaching. They must have known I was unsettled.

So I decided—I didn't go to university—I went down to St. Mary's College, which was then a Catholic training college. It's now a university. That was the year they were introducing the B.Ed., but instead of doing that, I did the other offering: a degree course combined with teacher training.

It was during this time that I completely forgot about being a priest. I finally began to enjoy a bit of life. But there's this underlying meaninglessness, this underlying boredom that comes because nothing you're doing is of any direct meaning to your own heart. And so what you overlay it with is distraction. You get drunk, you chase women, you do all sorts of daft things.
That went on for a little while until... It was during that time I fell in love. Oh my goodness. Well, you know what the first time is. So I'm completely sozzled, right?
And on one steamy, love-drenched night, she fell pregnant. This was my third catastrophe.

Now in a Catholic community, for that to happen is a horror story. The abortion law had just been introduced, so it was legal. Let's not confuse legal with moral, just because in a liberal society many of our personal decisions are left to us. But I just couldn't go there. Getting married and having children—it just wasn't on my to-do list.

So we struggled with it. We both struggled with it. She would have been, I think, happy going that way. Eventually I had to be true to my own heart. I went up to visit the parents and said, look, I can't marry you. I'll support them. It was a way of getting myself into a position where eventually I'd marry—an in-between place where I felt I wasn't entrapped, and then finally I'd probably give in.
But to the family, it just wasn't a go. They saw disaster for their daughter. They had connections with surgeons and such, and so she had an abortion.

I never felt really bad personally about that because I was never brought into that decision-making. And of course I'm part of that whole process. But if you go on the website and type in something like 'reactions to abortion,' you'll see pages and pages of men and women who really regret it—no matter what good reason they had, they actually regret that whole process.
So it's there, shall we say, within my psyche—that at some point in my life there was an abuse of my sexual powers, even though it was also a very wonderful moment.
Buddhism is no different from other religions on this point. As soon as there's conception, there's consciousness—a living being. There's no way out of that. That's what's understood, anyway.

What this did to me, I think, was further alienate me from my own heart. It put me deeper into a kind of underlying despair that I never really looked at—it was just there underneath. The boredom, the meaninglessness, and now this tragedy: all of it lay upon me as a sort of despair, and every occasion would tip me into an angry depression. But it never lasted long because I'm very sanguine.

And then one day... I was twenty-one. It was 1968—a very important date. That was the year of the student revolutions, if you remember, in Paris and all that. I lay in bed on a Sunday and said to myself, I wonder what will happen if I don't go to church.

Now in those days, if you didn't go to church it was a mortal sin. And if you died without confessing to a priest, you went to hell forever. That was the teaching. So it was a big decision. They changed all that in the mid-sixties with Pope John XXIII.
I said to myself, if I don't go to church today, let's see what happens. Nothing happened. So I never went again.

I know—it's the most ridiculous thing to do. What it really showed was that the religion had lost its connection to devotion. It had become an empty ritual: just turning up for mass, receiving communion, and so on. It had lost its potency. I think there might also have been guilt about the abortion. So there was a cutoff.
What this did, of course, was deepen my alienation to the whole thing. I found myself where Nietzsche found himself—I only discovered Nietzsche fairly recently—because God is dead. We've killed him.
Nietzsche struggled with that. He came up with a kind of courage to face the sufferings and death of life and somehow rise above it. That's what he meant by the superman or supraman, the Übermensch.

The Buddha himself, if you remember, had that sort of existential crisis. He puts it in the myth: seeing a sick person, an aging person, a corpse, and then this figure sitting under a tree. The whole thing is about him in his young twenties coming to realise that what he's heading towards is sickness, aging, and death—and that this image of the samaṇa, the recluse sitting under a tree, suggested some sort of escape.
So we have to imagine the Buddha himself going through an existential crisis. And it's so strong within him—the need to find an answer—that he leaves his family. In his case, his just-born child.
From our point of view, this is where the Buddha's at: the meaningfulness of life is gone. It's an existential angst. And that's where I was too, really—caught in this existential anxiety.

Now, I don't want to give you the impression that I was going around moping. No, I was still getting drunk and having a good time. It's just that every so often, in silent moments by yourself, you'd suddenly sink into this area of sheer boredom—a sort of despair about life. You're just living it.

And then came my third blessing. We went to see Beckett's *Waiting for Godot*. Some of you probably don't know that play because it's years old now. But it's basically about two tramps having these gorgeous conversations while waiting for Godot. Godot's going to come and sort it all out. And the same thing happens in the second act.

In the first act, two people come and walk across the stage—I won't go into too much detail. But in the second act, one's gone blind and the other's gone dumb. In the first act, there are leaves on the tree. In the second act, there are no leaves. So here are the two tramps waiting for Godot, and everything's just getting worse.

I came out of that play—even though it was just an amateur college production—absolutely enthused. I couldn't stop talking about it. They all thought I'd gone mad. But to me, it just caught this whole existential problem: what is the point if in the end you just die?
So let me just read this little poem to you, see what you think.
*Just think, if all this, one day, all this one day, one fine day, just think, if one day, one fine day, all this stopped.*

In terms of my emotional life, when I wasn't being extrovert and going out there, there was this—there's a lovely poem by Gerard Manley Hopkins that gets it across. I've carried this with me for a long time now. It gives you an idea. I think it's just a wonderful piece of poetry.

*I wake and feel the fell of dark, not day. / What hours, what black hours we have spent this night! / What sights, heart, you saw, ways you went, and more must in longer lights delay! / With witness I speak this, but where I say hours, I mean years, I mean life. / My lament is cries countless, cries like dead letters sent to dearest him, that lives, alas, away. / I am gall, I am heartburn. / God's most deep decree / Bitter would have me taste. / My taste was me. / Bones built in me, flesh filled, blood brimmed the curse. / Self-yeast of spirit, a dull dough sours. / I see the lost are like this, and their scourge to be as I am mine. / Their sweating selves, but worse.*
Ooh. Isn't that good, eh? *Their sweating selves.* I love it.

After that terrible birth, we seem to have both recovered. I hadn't seen this picture for years, and when I saw it, I thought, well, blow me down. Two things came to mind. First, we remember our mothers as old, in old age. We forget they were all lovely young women.

The other thing was that little child—if it was just shown to me, there's no way I'd make a connection with that child at all. This is my tenth body, if we're to believe biology. Every ten years, you completely change all the atoms in your body.

So okay, I leave. The relationship breaks up—I can't handle it anymore. Anyway, I'm in Spain looking for my friends and I've lost them. I've arrived there sometime later. And I pick up a telegram from the local post office. Do you remember telegrams? Yeah, fantastic. You get it out through the telegram.
It said, 'Congratulations, son—BA and teaching certificate.' I climbed up onto a high rock overlooking the sea and said to myself: there's no way in this world I want to teach.

I went down and camped at a site nearby. That night there was torrential rain. I woke up with the tent collapsed around me, completely sodden with sand and everything, and staggered to the drinks area—what do you call it? The bar, where people drink and stuff like that. I was looking pretty miserable.
There I met this bloke called Dave. We began talking, and by the end of the evening we'd decided to travel together across the world. Simple as that, see?

So I came back and got a job through somebody I knew as a rep selling audiovisuals to schools. There was lots of money in education, so I made lots of money, travelling all over Cornwall and Wales selling this stuff. I gathered something like a thousand pounds—which must be what, ten thousand today? A lot of money.

Come the year, off we went. Remember, all this time I've got this thing going on—this nothingness and this stuff in the heart. But because of the joy of travel, the constant new information coming in and the liberation of it, all that was sort of underneath. It would only come up in the gloomier moments.

We started off towards North Africa, went down and saw all the stuff in Egypt—the usual, you know, ride a camel and all that. Went over to Khartoum, lay on the luggage rack to sleep and woke up with sand everywhere because of a sandstorm.

We got onto a boat down to Juba, which is now the capital of South Sudan. For ten days we were looking at the amazing sight of reeds. The reeds were so tall they went up above the boat, and the boat just ploughed through this canal, which was the Nile. All you saw for ten days was reeds. And because we didn't have much money, we were just eating bread. So this was a really gloomy time—that's when I wrote most of my doggerel about nothingness.

We got down to Juba, flew across to Entebbe, then made this amazing bus ride across Africa. I slept on the back seat, and every so often I was in midair as the driver ran over an elephant. It was just unbelievable.

We got to Mombasa and stayed with Dave's brother, but we didn't get on so well, so we went up the beach and camped there. That's where we got robbed—all our traveller's cheques were stolen. Which was actually fantastic, because when you got to India you could report them stolen, get them replaced, and make a lot of money on the black market. Terrible, isn't it?

They also stole my father's watch. He'd given me this gold watch that he'd been given after finishing his time as headmaster at St Patrick's. It had an inscription on the back—a precious thing, you know?
So I wrote him a letter expressing my sense of sorrow and my love for him and all that. Well, when his reply came back about three or four months later, he said: 'Thank you, son. Bit sentimental.'

So we get to India. We took this cattle boat across the Indian Ocean from Mombasa to Bombay, playing chess all the time. Lots of travellers, by the way. There weren't many going down through Egypt, but by the time we got to Africa the boat was full of Westerners. All hippies. I was dressed in purple.

We took that boat all the way across to Bombay and did the usual trip, finally ending up in Nepal. That's when we got really stoned. Up in Nepal there were lots of these stones full of dope, and these lovely hippie gatherings where we'd all sit in a circle with a chillum. Ridiculous, isn't it?
That lasted for a while, and then we made our way down to Calcutta.
Calcutta was at war. This was the Indo-Pakistan war where Bangladesh split off, so there were no planes going out. We were stuck there for two weeks.

What I did every day during that time became one of my big awakenings. When you went to India—this time it wasn't so bad—but in those days, sickness and destitution were in your face. Right in your face.

In Calcutta they have a huge park with this massive statue of Queen Victoria, which I presume they've got rid of now. A circuit road runs around it, and that road was packed tight with the sick. Elephantiasis, everything apart from lepers, all the way around. They were lying on the pavement. When you saw that, you understood Mother Teresa's compassion. Because many of them would have died just there.
I saw that, but that sort of thing I wasn't used to. I wasn't ready to receive it. I was aware of the suffering, but I wasn't ready to receive it.

Every day I went to this Sikh restaurant to get curry, and I would always ask for a glass of water. When they brought it—a pint glass—you'd always see a nice oily thumbprint on the side. I don't know how it was that I was in India for so long and never caught anything. Just weird.

Every day I passed this woman who had a huge goitre. She sat there absolutely silent, holding a little thing for coins, her eyes always glazed with tears. She never looked at me, just sat there. Day after day I passed that poor woman on the other side of the road, and I never gave her a penny.

Then I'm coming round a corner, just wandering around Calcutta, and right before me there's this little girl. She must be over seven, but she looks about five. She's sucking a dried black banana peel. She sees me, and as she sees me, she has the dignity in that destitution to cover her sex.
That hit me so hard I just bombed past her. I never went back to buy her a banana. It just hit me so hard.
That was my little boy drowned on the beach moment—where suddenly, the awareness of compassion is not enough. There has to be that contact.

Anyway, that passed. But it stays with me. Every time I bring that image to mind, if I feel callous about anything—about refugees and all these people—I just bring that little child to mind. That's my connection with all the suffering that's going on these days.

So we're in Calcutta, and we did a couple of naughty things. We had opium. Opium's wonderful. I was laid out there all night, and I remember waking up and watching all these cockroaches going over Dave's body. I went into the toilet, and there were cockroaches everywhere. It sounded like a cockroach. I thought, fantastic.

I looked around, and all my trouble was gone. What is all this business? Nothing. It's stupid. Get rid of it. I was absolutely blissed out. Then, of course, it wears off, and I'm depressed and anxious again.

Before long, we're off on a flight to Bangkok, doing the usual tourist trip, wandering into all these beautiful Buddhist buildings. That's it. I had no connection at all with Buddhism. For me, they were just museums.

It was only years later, when I read Emotional Intelligence by Goleman—the book that brought this whole thing about emotional intelligence into consciousness—that I understood something. He says if you're a depressive, you tend to be drawn to those sorts of drugs that lift you out of your depression. These aren't hallucinatory drugs. And if you're an anxious person, you're drawn to alcohol. Well, that's me, isn't it?

We left Bangkok, went down through Malaysia, and then had to walk across the bridge to Singapore. When we got to the checkpoint, they sent us back to get a haircut. So we had to cut my hair off, all of it, and then come back. Then we took this boat—a real proper lovely little boat—all the way to Port Moresby. It was on its way to Cairns and down to Australia. And that was New Guinea.

We flew from New Guinea to Madang, and I took up my post as a teacher in the Society of the Divine Word Missionary School. Yes. I'd contacted them again and got this job—myself and Dave. He taught art and I was teaching English.

That was a great year, teaching those kids. They actually want to learn. And there were great things like climbing Mount Hagen and going up this boat run by a priest who was really a dislocated cowboy. He went up and down barking orders in that strident American way, giving sermons, selling things, and shooting crocodiles. He had his huge rifle. Bang—shoot.
It was during that time that, because I was settled—and as soon as I was settled, I've got this dreadful thing going on, this nothingness.

I go back from teaching one day, I don't know what it was, I lie on the bed and I'm suddenly suffused with this enormous fear. And for something—I don't know, I must have got it from somewhere—I turn towards the fear. I go into it, and next minute, I'm out of my body. I'm up there looking down. I turn round, I see that tunnel, that swirling tunnel, and I'm back into the body.
All fear of death disappeared. Not the fear of dying—all fear of death disappeared. And suddenly, out of the blue, I start writing plays. I start writing plays.

I went down to Australia because I was interested in theatre. I went to a training school really to learn the art of theatre because I knew virtually nothing about it. And that's where I fall in love again. Hijacked.

So we have a lovely relationship. It's going fine. We're involved in theatre. I get one or two plays going. And suddenly this idea of marriage comes up. My brother had just got married. I was twenty-seven. And that's what you do.
But in my heart of hearts, I wasn't with it at all. Because of the relationship, because of the love and all that, you get married. And so we went through a civil service.

There was something in me that just couldn't handle this. I was getting overly happy. And I remember my dear best man, as we got into the taxi, said to me, "Pete" — that's what I was called — "Pete, you've got to take something seriously."

Well, it was a disaster. Somebody who had been a friend, a companion, a lover, somebody I shared theatre with — she suddenly became a burden. I'd fallen into the patriarchal trap. She became a burden.

We went on a honeymoon and I drained it of joy. At one point she said to me, "If you carry on like this, I'll leave you." That's not the sort of conversation you have on a honeymoon, is it? It's absolutely awful.

We got to London, and my brother offered us lodgings. Hitting London in November from Australia — it was the beginning of summer there — the rain and the cold, and then we hit the theatre, and it was just overwhelming. Sydney was a small city with a major theatre and a couple of little outlets. You hit London, and it's just impossible.
I sent my plays off and followed them up. One came back with "600" written on it. It was just overwhelming. I was so depressed, and I was dragging her down too.

We were at SOAS, the School of Oriental and African Studies. While we were there, a Nigerian approached us and said he was taking a troupe out to Nigeria — would we be interested? We were completely lost. We just said yes.

He offered us lodgings. "I'm going to get the group together in a house," he said. So I left my brother's, went to this house, and it was just an empty room with a mattress in it. We had to put a line up for the clothes. I'm standing there for two, three weeks, and nothing's happening. We keep saying, "Larry, can you show us the play?"

Finally he shows us this play, and it could have been written by a ten-year-old. It was an inverted racist play — all about this great black chief who was going to teach these stupid white people something. Myself and Chris — by the way, her mother was Aboriginal and her father was English — we just looked at each other: got to get out of this.

Very fortunately, I'd lost contact with Dave, my travelling companion. And as we got onto a tube, he was right there. I said, "Dave!" See? Just a wonderful thing. He was moving out of a flat in Highgate. We were down in Kentish Town. And it just worked for us.

The night before, we'd planned the escape — there must have been something in us that told us something like this would happen. We told Larry we weren't going to come. That evening he came round and went ballistic. It was racist. It was awful. My wife stepped forward and slapped his face. He made for her. I grabbed hold of him. I'm on the floor with him, holding him down, shouting, "Relax, Larry, let go." Finally, when he gives up the struggle, we both get up and he's standing there and he goes off again. I'm very penitent, saying, "Get back." Finally he left.
That night, we slept with chairs up against the doors. I slept with a carving knife. It's the case, it's everyday.

Seven o'clock in the morning, we were in that taxi and off we went. Things just got worse after that — worse and worse. Eventually, my wife got a job with a theatre company up in Birmingham. And really, that was the first leaving. We both knew something was wrong, knew it wasn't working. Her leaving was the beginning of it — of her leaving me.

I went back home really depressed. I was teaching FE again. It was awful. I was getting nowhere with my plays. There was a fantastic opportunity when the director of Earl's Court Theatre actually asked me to come along. I had an interview with him, and we were discussing a play I'd written. You don't do that with a top director. But I didn't have the energy to go back with another play. I didn't keep the contact. That's what you do — once you've got your foot in, you keep pushing. And I didn't.
So I'm getting slowly, slowly depressed, like a play.

That's when Chris tells me she's fallen in love with somebody else. Apart from the ego thump, the affection had almost disappeared, really. So there wasn't that sense of loss you might get from somebody you truly love.

But it put a cap on my situation. I was getting nowhere, the plays were no good, I was teaching — which I hated — and I was going nowhere. I was in a state of absolute despair. Shock and despair. Everything I'd built up these last years had just collapsed.
And then it came to me that the most rational thing to do, the most obvious thing to do — in fact, it was common sense — was to go to Japan to meditate.

I don't know how that came. I'd read a book on Zen Buddhism in Sydney, and I'd walked from where I lived to the shops, looking at myself, observing myself. By the time I got to the shops, I thought, no, this is going to drive me crazy. So that was it. That was my Zen. That was all I knew.

I wrote to the Buddhist Society. How did we find places before Google? It's a mystery, isn't it? Anyway, I wrote to them, and they wrote back with twenty-one reasons why I shouldn't go to Japan. But they put me in touch with Throssel Hole Abbey, which had only recently opened up in Hexham in Northumberland. And from there, they put me in touch with Vajra Bailey, who was teaching in Birmingham — just a bus ride away from where I was living.

I remember going there on a cold February day. She was very Zen. The house was cold. We got up to the room, and there were a couple of Greek people there. Then she took us into the shrine room. We all bowed, and I thought, this is ridiculous.
Then she sat me on a stool, pushed my back in, and sat me upright. She said, just watch. Keep your eyes open like that. The instruction was: watch what comes up.
I sat there for forty minutes in absolute agony, both emotionally and physically. But by the end of it, I was convinced this was going to get me out of this hole.

She had a room in the house that wasn't occupied, so I went to lodge with her. That was February. By June, by July, I had a job — a really easy one, teaching English to foreign students. And I bought a house. Everything began to move in that way. That was my last catastrophe, my last big catastrophe. I've had catastrophes since, but they're only small Cs.

I carried on with the practice. Then I went up to the Zen monastery, and being part of that community was like being back at St Richard's. It came to me with such ease. I loved going up there, even during the summer holidays—I'd spend six weeks at Throssel Abbey.

Then one day I came back, lay on the bed, and this tremendous fear filled the whole of my body, every cell—I felt it everywhere. And there came this picture of a little boy, two years old, going into the front room, shaking my grandfather and saying: he's dead, they've killed him, now they're going to kill me. Two years old.
I said to my father, 'I found grandfather dead in the front room.' He said, 'No, he died in bed. I was with him.'

This tells you a lot about trauma, children's trauma. Obviously my parents weren't getting on—they didn't. And I'm thinking I'm the problem. They go in, and I presume they've killed the old man, my dear old grandfather. And I presume they're going to kill me now.

You have to be very careful with childhood memories, because you don't know what you're laying on them in the remembering. I speak to myself in English, but I don't think I had English at the age of two—I could barely speak. But that was the thought.

What happened then was I got a series of images coming through my life, showing me how this trauma had cancered every one of my relationships. There's your karmic line. It was just so plain in the face, unbelievable.

Shortly after that—I mean, that was such a wonderful relief—and then a little while later, I'm walking along the road, and on the other side there's a copse of wood. As I turn into the copse, I get this thump on the head. This energy comes down into the heart, shoots out towards a tree, and what I'm looking at is a kaleidoscopic vortex of energy reaching up into the sky.
For a while I was blissed out. I remember particularly looking at the crimson geometry of a rose. That lasted for an hour or two, and then I got depressed and anxious again.

Now if you were to ask me, if I had a choice between those two—what would I have gone for? This sort of vision, or this purification of the heart? No choice. You go for the purification of the heart. Because that's something you can do. You can't make these other things happen. And they seem to come to cap a certain level. They don't come to begin something—they seem to end a process.
I don't think that sort of insight or vision, whatever it was, would have been possible without the clearing of the heart.
If you look at the Dhammapada, the Buddha puts the practice as: stop doing evil, do good, purify the heart. This is the teaching of all the Buddhas. It's as simple as that.
This purifying of the heart, what we go through when we're sitting there with all these things coming up—that is something we can actually do something about directly.
Now when it comes to this other thing, this bump on the head—what do you think? God? Some big being? Or just neurons firing through your head, just atomic and subatomic activity?

The thing is, I don't know. It didn't feel like a personal God. It just felt as though I was given this gift, and I've remained at that level. If something like that happens to you, you don't have to expand it into some huge cosmology. It's just something you receive. Just an interesting thing.

What I understood from it was that the whole of this phenomenal world is nothing but light—nothing but energy. Matter is just the coagulation of it. That's all it is. That's what it's made of. There's nothing real, nothing substantial in it.

Anyway, all that passed, and I carried on teaching. The story from here is basically how I became a Buddhist. I went up to Throssel and joined the Zen monastery. Through that, through Vajra Bailey, I met the Theravāda monk and did a retreat with him. And in that retreat I was drawn.

The next year the Mahāsī teachers came, and that gave me a real technique to delve into myself in a way that Zen hadn't really done. It was from that experience that I decided to take a sabbatical from work, and I went to Burma with my teacher for six months.

During that time, this whole business of impermanence became even clearer. There I actually saw that although what I'd understood about the tree might be true out there, what's actually true in here is the arising and passing away, minutely, of consciousnesses. That's what we're experiencing all the time—just this screen flashing, flashing inside us.

And what is it that's aware of this? What is it that's looking at it? That, of course, is our satipaññā, our intuitive awareness. And it's that separation which is the process of liberating ourselves from the delusion of thinking that we are this psychophysical organism.

When I got back, I was really fired up. I was going to make a go of it. I returned to the college I was teaching at, and they gave me a promotion for my MSc. Can you believe that? I thought, this is what I've got to do—take a sabbatical every so often, come back, and get a promotion.

During that time, I was ready to move into the whole thing of marriage. I bought a new house, a semi-detached. And I began to fall in love again. Oh, my goodness. So there was this real potential, you see.
But something else started to creep in: why don't I spend three years as a monk?

The monastic order was established by the Buddha exactly for that reason. When you look at the rules of the monastic order, you end up simply learning the Dhamma or meditating. There's nothing beyond that if you're keeping the rule as he would want it. And the whole infrastructure that comes with it in a society holds you there, holds you in that place.
I struggled with that for about a year, making lists for and against, for and against.

Finally, when it really got on top of me, I went to see Rewata Dhamma, my teacher. I said, "Bhante, I want to become a monk." And he said, "You're too old." What a relief. Right, let's get back and live properly.

So that was it. But it wouldn't leave me. Another six months passed, and I was back to him saying, "Listen, I've got to—I really have to ordain. Even if it's only for three years." That was my intention: a three-year ordination.

Again he said no—I was needed there. By this time I'd become so much part of that vihāra. And there were only a few of us. Rewata Dhamma wasn't the type of person who collected people, so there were only about three of us holding the whole place up.

Again I went away, but this time I wasn't so convinced. I spent two weeks in meditation with a good friend who read Lord of the Rings while I was suffering in the other room. If you're listening, Peter, you'll remember. I came back absolutely determined. And when I went to see Rewata Dhamma again, he said, "OK."

So I ordained for three years. I remember telling my mother. She burst into this enormous veil of tears. I put my arm around her and said, "Mum, it's only for three years." And she stopped. You see, in Catholicism, if you're a monk, you disappear. These days they're not so strict, but back then you disappeared into the monastery—you didn't see him again.

There was a lovely feast for that ordination. Then I went off to work with Goenkaji at his centre in Igatpuri, India. And there, something came out of my neck—some snake or other—it was absolutely awful. I came back feeling I'd got rid of some big thing.
And who should be there at the vihāra but Sayadaw U Pandita—some of you might know him. He's one of the prime teachers of the Mahasi tradition. He invited me to come and join him in Burma.

Before I did that, I thought: why don't I go and live at Amaravati, the big monastery, and really get to know what it is to live in that kind of community? So I turned up there. It was during that year that there were those demonstrations in Burma—all the killing and everything they did. It was the one before the uprising that finally made the military pull back. So I decided not to go.

Then at the turning of the year, I said to the sangha, to the monks: listen, I ought to go and tell Sayadaw U Pandita why I've not turned up. So I went to his retreat at IMS—that's the Insight Meditation Society in Barre, Massachusetts.

As soon as I walked into the meditation hall, I just knew this was it. I didn't want to live in that sort of forest community type of monasticism. I wanted this hard grind—full-on, full metal jacket meditation.

So I decided to go to Burma. But I couldn't get in. I got to Bangkok, couldn't get a visa, and ended up in a monastery in the north. I finally caught up with U Pandita down in Penang and did a retreat with him there.
By this time I was despairing again. It had been four years. I wasn't getting anywhere. I'd determined three years, so I thought: I'll give it one more.
So I'm kneeling in front of the Buddha statue, and I'm saying: look, I've done what I can. If nothing happens in another year, I'm going to go back and do something useful.
There was a German monk there I was talking to, pouring out all my sorrows, telling him where I was. And he said to me, why don't you go to Sri Lanka? Sri Lanka? I'd never even thought of the place.
So I wrote to Venerable Pemasiri, who was the abbot, and he wrote back and said, come. When I got there, I told him what I wanted. I said, I just want to sit. I just want to find somewhere I can sit.

He gave me some keys and directed me to a kuti, a hut at the back of the monastery. When I got there, I found this beautiful little ten-foot by six-foot hut with a caṅkama—a walking area with walls to keep the snakes out—and a garden all to myself. I walked in and thought, this is absolutely marvellous.

I had a toilet built—that was important. And then came that day, which is very rare even in the monastic life, where you don't go into retreat with the idea that you're going to finish in a month, three months, four months. I went in with no ending date.
So I sat there going through all this purification of the heart, doing the meditation from two o'clock till ten o'clock at night, really going for it. I was absolutely empowered.

It was during that time my second trauma appeared out of the blue. I had this dream of looking across a pond, and there was a ghost waving at me. Now this ghost was exactly what you'd expect—a big sheet with two eyes—and it was on the other side of the pond. I was absolutely terrified. I asked a Jungian psychologist about it later. He said it was a birth trauma. I don't know.

The dream came once, it came twice, this nightmare. Then one night I woke up and there it was in the corner of the room. I remember bouncing on the bed in absolute terror, and my little voice saying, what do you want? What do you want? The thing just disappeared. It fizzled out. And that was it—something just came out of the heart, this trauma. I still don't know what it means.
After that, the clarity of impermanence became even more obvious to me. And it was so fortunate. Death doesn't particularly regard you. Death happens when it wants to. It's usually most inconvenient.

At that point, just when everything was finished, my father died. I remember receiving the news and walking back towards the kuti, crying. But I didn't cry long, because when I reflected on his life, he had a good life. He was a happy man, very sanguine. And I loved him much.

I went back for his funeral. Then I thought, somebody has to stay with my mother, comfort her. So I stayed with her for a year, and I was a real Job's comforter—I think I caused her more misery. Death stirs things up. I mean, if there's one woman I love to bitch about, it's my mother. I could strangle her.
I stayed with her for one year, then came back and said to the family, I don't think she's going to live long because she doesn't have the moral support.

So I went back to Sri Lanka with fierce intention, ready to start again. But it wasn't long before my mother became seriously ill. I returned to England, and this time I felt really bad about that year of so-called comforting. This time I made a real effort.

You couldn't live with my mother—it was impossible. So we got her into a lovely nursing home. At first she hated it. She talked about le carrozze, these wheelchairs. She couldn't stand the wheelchairs. But when I went to see her after about a month, she was perfectly at home. She was back in boarding school, surrounded by all these friends, all these women, chatting away. She was the life of the party. It was absolutely wonderful to see her.
And again, most convenient, she decided to have a stroke just before I was due to leave for Sri Lanka. She died within three days. So having buried her, I went back and started the process again.

The death of my mother was really weird. I felt like an orphan. I was, what, forty-five years old? And I felt like an orphan. I wrote this big, long letter to one of my cousins in Italy explaining how awful I felt. I don't know what he's done with it.
That passed. And then I went back and really went for it again.

There came a point where it was just impossible. The restlessness, the pain that was coming out of me—it was constant. Day in, day out. I woke up into it, carried it through the day, slept into it, woke up and it just went on. Day after day. It got to a point when I thought, no, I just can't go on. I can't go on. I'm going to stop.
I can't tell you what happened then. When I gave up at that moment, there came into me this enormous terror, which picked me up and threw me back onto the meditation cushion.

Because that's the last thing you do. You can stop for a while, you can leave it, you can get drunk, you can do anything—but you never give up. Once you're on the path, you move on the path. If you give up, there's only one way to go. You're going to go down.

I was horrified. I can't tell you the horror that struck me when I realised I'd actually given up on my spiritual journey. So I got over that pretty quickly. I just sat on the cushion and mowed my way through it.
By then my body was beginning to suffer, I think. I was feeling weak, not strong. I wrote to a monk I knew in London, Dr Vajrayana, with the intention of staying there just to get my body right.

So I turned up at the London Buddhist Vihara. I had this back room, and I was basically meditating. Then the monk who was teaching there decided to leave the order and get married. So who did they ask to sit on the podium? Me.

So I'm sitting there, and I'm slowly coming to understand that whatever took me to Sri Lanka—that energy, that inspiration—was dying away. I didn't want to go back. But how would I live in Britain? The only way you can live in Britain, if you want to find somewhere spiritually supportive, is to begin to teach. I needed support. So that's where the teaching began. But of course I had this horror of teaching. I really didn't want to do it.
So I'm teaching weekend retreats, and come the year 2000, I decide I've got to sort this out. I go on a retreat with Sayadaw U Pandita, outside his place, Panditarama, in the middle of Rangoon.

When you go on retreat with a problem, you don't think about the problem. You just go on retreat. And whatever is causing the problem will, in fact, sort itself out. What's normally causing the problem is confusion. If you just sit with it—as confusion, as fear of making a decision—eventually it sorts itself out.
That was another rotten retreat. Six weeks of misery. And then when it passed, it was lovely and peaceful for a week.

Towards the end of the retreat I'm in the hall, looking at the bookcase, and my eye falls on this book. It's all the stuff on teaching taken out of A Course in Miracles. Some of you might know that book—it's a big book and it's got a teacher's book with it.

I pick it out and start reading, and by the end of that book I'm absolutely certain that what I've got to do is teach. How weird. It happens, doesn't it? You've got this problem, you call for help, and the answer comes. It comes in its own time.
So I get back to Britain, go back to the Vihara, set up a little trust. And before we know it, we've collected twenty-five thousand pounds to open a centre.

And that's where the big mistake comes. I open up in the north of London, but all my support is in the south. I completely forget that London is not a city—it's a collection of villages. You don't go from the south to the north for an evening meeting. That's a two-hour journey.
On the opening day, thirty-odd people came. And what happened was they never came again. They never came again, and that's it.

Come September, I said to Mattesh—a Dutch student doing a PhD at one of the London universities, a lovely man who supported me all the way—if we don't have somebody by the end of September, we're not going to have a group.

So we advertised all over the place, made these little stickers. I had two young fellas, and they cycled everywhere putting them up—laundrettes, libraries, everywhere. And they had the wrong telephone number. Yes, you learn, you learn.
The first night the neighbour came, and he never came again. The second night nobody came. By October I'm thinking this is a disaster.

By the end of that season the Sri Lankans had come to help us, especially Sita—Dr. Sita Sriwarni. She was always there helping me. Then we had this six-week retreat over winter. We closed the place down. There were five of us. And I decided it was not going to work. I've got to get out.

Luckily, one of the retreatants, Peter, knew the Batchelors—Stephen and Martine. Some of you might have read their books. He got in touch with them and told them that the next cosmic Buddha was in north London. So there you go.

They came up to see me, and it happened to be one of those lovely times when Gaia House was stuck. The Batchelors had moved to France, and they needed somebody to look after the personal retreatants—about twelve of them.

I went to see Christina Feldman, and I thought it was a great place. When I was there, it really became my Dharma college. Not only was I working with people who had come on personal retreat—some were ill with ME and things like that, some had psychological problems, not mental illnesses but passing depressions and lots of stuff—I also had to deal with some of the managers running the place. And I was given the chance to run eight-week retreats in the Mahasi system twice a year.
It was during that time I realised there was a little space to start something like this.

After about a year or two, I decided to leave. I was never really fully part of the teachers' organisation there because it's a very lay organisation. Again, I came to a choice: disrobe and become a lay teacher, or remain a monk. It was a bit like—for those of you who know the imagery—being a Catholic priest amongst Quakers.

So I decided to leave. Then we went through the whole business of setting up a trust. I have a special thanks to Anne Ashton, who was the accounts person at Gaia House. She took us through the whole process with a lawyer we got from down in London—a simple letter to the charitable trust and we had it. It only cost us two and a half thousand pounds.
Well, then it's just history, isn't it? I found this place, and from then on I think it's all about...
So you might ask now, after all this: where am I? Everybody wants to know where you are.

The Buddha gives this image of an ocean in which beings are swimming underneath, completely lost. Others bob their head up and down. Some are stable, and they can see there's an island—an island which is both in, but not of, this world.
Now for me, it wasn't an ocean—it's been a swamp. I'm stuck in this swamp with crocodiles, and every so often there's a bit of a clearing. That's it.

All I can say is that I know where the island is. That's the important thing. I know where the island is. Does that mean I'm making a beeline for it? Not at all. I slip over here, slip over there, sometimes turn my back on it. But generally speaking, I'm on the way. That's where you've got to get to.

You can see how I just stumbled and bumbled and blundered my way through life—but not without lots of summery somersaults and sticky toffee pudding with custard. And I've landed here in an area of outstanding natural beauty. It's not bad, is it?

Now there are two things I want to say. First, I've shared some of my spiritual experiences because I want to release people from keeping theirs to themselves. Meditators often come to me and tell me things similar to what I've told you. When I ask, 'Have you told anybody?' they say, 'No, it's just for me.' There isn't a framework within which they feel free to express themselves.

And often, of course, when they can't talk about these experiences, they get into what's known as a spiritual emergency—it sends them offline and they end up with a doctor. If the doctor's not sympathetic, you're back on drugs. It gets labelled a psychotic interlude or something.

So be much more open about out-of-body experiences. When I go to Gaia House, I'll occasionally ask a group of twenty-five how many have had out-of-body experiences. Five hands go up. I wouldn't say that's the same percentage as the general population, but it's not an unknown experience.
And so finally—what is it I would really like to pass on to you? What is it I've come to know?

I've come to realise that the difference between me and the Buddha is one of degree, not of kind. And the difference between me and you is the same. And the difference between us and all sentient beings, all these animals—if you look into their eyes, the mirrors, the windows to the soul—you'll see they have this intelligence.

Because of that, the force that lies in us all is to seek happiness. And because we're deluded, we create all this suffering. But at some point there has to be a turning. You turn round upon it, and at some point in your life you're pushed by your own suffering to move towards the end of it.

Now Kant has this little phrase: a categorical imperative. He's talking about the moral law, whereby if you make a moral law—like not to steal—that law is unconditionally and completely applicable to all human beings. In the category of stealing, it is an imperative, a command that all human beings obey. You can't say one human being can steal and others can't.
So I would like to say that it is a categorical imperative that all beings, all sentient beings, will become fully liberated from all suffering forever. Absolutely.

I can only hope my words have been of some assistance, that they haven't caused confusion and befuddlement, and that you will—by your devotion to the practice, unrelenting, unremitting devotion to the practice—come to the end of suffering forever, sooner rather than later.
Sādhu, sādhu, sādhu.
